topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the schematic spread across the metal table, the fluorescent lights buzzing overhead amplifying the silence in the lab. She traced a line on the blueprint with a fingertip, then lifted her gaze to the adjacent workstation. Across the narrow aisle, Liam was leaning over his own identical document, his brow furrowed in concentration. The complexity of the interlocking mechanisms, the precise calibration points—they were a mirror image. A cold prickle began at the base of Elara's neck. She shifted in her chair, the squeak of the worn vinyl protesting the movement. Liam didn't look up. He was meticulously adjusting a tiny resistor, his movements fluid and practiced. "That's… strange," Elara managed, her voice sounding thin in the space. Liam finally paused, his head tilting slightly. He looked up, and for a fleeting second, their eyes met. There was a flicker of something unreadable in his expression, quickly masked by professional detachment.

[story 2]

"It's impossible," Marcus muttered, tapping his pen against the data log. He glanced over at Chloe, who was engrossed in her own progress. They were both attempting to solve the same differential equation, the same structural design for the prototype. The sheer coincidence felt heavy, pressing down on his chest. He leaned back, running a hand through his already messy hair, the movement jerky. He watched Chloe meticulously annotate her notes, the ink flowing in identical, elegant script. Every shared notation, every calculated risk, felt like an intrusion. Marcus cleared his throat, a dry, rasping sound. "Did you happen to see… anything unusual on your side?" Chloe looked up, her expression carefully neutral, yet her eyes held a guarded quality. "Just the usual," she replied, her tone carefully level. But Marcus could see the slight tension around her jaw, the way she avoided prolonged eye contact.

[story 3]

I found the finished prototype resting on the workbench, and then I saw it: the identical packaging mock-up sitting beside it. My breath hitched. It was the same iteration, the same subtle flaw in the casing design, the same branding placement. I stood frozen, my hands gripping the edge of the steel counter until my knuckles turned white. Across the room, David was packing up his tools, humming a tuneless melody. He finally noticed me, and his smile, usually so easy, faltered. He gave a small, hesitant wave. "Rough night?" he asked, his voice pitched a little too high. I couldn't formulate a response. My mouth felt dry, and I took a slow, deliberate step backward, wanting desperately to melt into the shadows of the corner, away from the undeniable symmetry of the situation.
